segments.s356-g57: همی functions as a habitual/progressive adverb ('still/always') marking ongoing action; چشم دارد is a literal 'has an eye' idiom meaning 'keeps watch' (دارَد is 3rd-person present of داشتن 'to have'); زریر و سپاه is the compound subject ('Zarir and the army'), following the adverbial phrase and linked to the verb دارد.
segments.s356-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/so that'); آیی is a present/subjunctive-like form from آمدن meaning 'you come' or 'may you come' expressing the expected action; خرامان is an adverbial/participial description 'striding proudly' modifying آیی; ب + دین here form بدین = 'to this' (prepositional phrase) and رزمگاه is the place-object 'battlefield' governed by that preposition.
segments.s356-g59: همه 'everyone' is the subject; سربسر is an intensifying adverb meaning 'entirely/throughout' modifying the verb phrase; با تو پیمان کنند is the verb phrase 'make a pact with you' (پیمان کردن + با + object), and کنند is the 3rd-person plural present/optative form of کردن used for 'they make/will make'.
segments.s356-g60: روان را is the definite-direct-object phrase ('the soul' with the object marker را); به مهرت is a prepositional phrase 'to/for your مهر' where مهرت is 'your love/affection' (the -ت is the 2nd-person possessive); گروگان کنند is the verb 'make (into) a hostage/pledge' with کنند again 3rd-plural, so the whole expresses 'they pledge/surrender the soul as a hostage to your love.'
segments.s356-g61: گرت is a conditional marker ('if it be that / if you') contracted from اگر + ت; رنج means 'suffering' or 'harm'; ناید here is the negative present/impersonal sense 'does not come / does not befall' (from آمدن); خرامی is the manner ('proud stride' used adverbially 'as you stride proudly'); به دشت is the prepositional phrase 'into / on the plain.' The clause thus means 'if suffering does not come to you while you stride on the plain.'
segments.s356-g62: که links the result clause ('then/so that'); کار زمانه is a noun phrase 'the affairs/work of the age' where زمانه carries the sense of fortune/age; به کام تو is an idiom meaning 'to your desire / in your favour' (به + کام + possessive تو); گشت is the past/resultive verb 'became/turned' (3rd-singular), so the clause states that the fortunes of the time have turned in your favour.
segments.s356-g63: فرستاده 'the messenger/envoy' is the subject; چون here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; نزد + قیصر means 'in the presence of Caesar' (نزد used as 'before/in the presence of'); رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached.' The sequence yields 'when the envoy reached Caesar.'
segments.s356-g64: به دشت آمد is a simple past clause 'he came to the plain' (به = 'to', دشت = 'plain', آمد = 'came'); و links the next action; ساز لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the army's saz' (here 'saz' refers to the musical/tactical array or martial music of the troops) and بدید is the past tense 'saw' (3rd-singular), so the second clause means 'and he saw the army's music/array.'
segments.s356-g113: همی + verb is an archaic/poetic continuous marker: همی راند = 'he rode on / kept riding'. تا + verb (تا ... رسید) here marks a temporal/limit meaning 'until' (he rode until he reached). سوی ایران is the directional adjunct 'toward Iran' (سوی = toward; ایران = the land). رسید is a simple past/perfective 'arrived/reached.'
segments.s356-g114: به نزد is a compound prepositional phrase 'to / in the presence of' (به = to; نزد = in the presence of). دلیران و شیران is a coordinated noun phrase 'the brave and the lions/lion‑like (i.e. heroic men)'; شیران here is figurative for brave men. رسید repeats the verbal idea 'arrived / came.'
segments.s356-g115: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' بشنید is past 'he heard.' In this poetic word order the report of arrival follows: کامد زریر = 'Zarir came' (کامد is an archaic/past form 'came'). The clause as a whole: 'When Lohrasp heard, Zarir came.'
segments.s356-g116: برادرش is 'his brother' (برادر + pronominal suffix ش). It is followed by the name گشتاسپ in apposition, then the epithet آن نره شیر ('that manly lion')—آن functions as a demonstrative/epithet and نره شیر is a fixed laudatory epithet meaning 'manly, lion‑like (i.e. heroic).' The whole phrase apposes Gushasp to the previous clause.
segments.s356-g117: پذیره شدش is a poetic past‑passive/experiential construction meaning 'he was received / welcomed' (پذیره = reception/accepted; شدش = befell him/was to him). با همه مهتران means 'with all the مهتران' (مهتران = chiefs/elders; the lemma list is irregular but here it denotes leading men).
segments.s356-g118: بزرگان ایران و نام‌آوران is a further specification/list: 'the great men of Iran and the famed (name‑making) ones' — both plural noun phrases in apposition to the chiefs. The coordination links the social groups who received him.
segments.s356-g119: چو دید introduces a temporal clause 'when he saw.' او پسر را = 'his son' with را marking the definite/direct object (noun before را). به بر درگرفت is an idiomatic poetic sequence: به بر = 'into his arms / to his bosom' and درگرفت (here used transitively with that phrase) means 'seized / took (to his breast)' — an old/poetic verb usage for embracing or taking up.
segments.s356-g120: ز + noun here expresses cause: ز جور فلک = 'because of the oppression/cruelty of Fate' (جور = injustice/hardship; فلک in such contexts often means 'fate/destiny'). دست بر سر گرفت is a conventional phrase 'he put his hand on his head' (دست = hand; بر = on/upon; سر = head), where گرفت is simple past 'took/placed (lit. seized)'; the gesture indicates lamentation or grief.
segments.s356-g33: به پیری (literally 'into/at old age') is an adverbial time phrase placed before the verb; ورا = 'to him' is the indirect object pronoun following the time phrase. بخت خندان شدست is a nominal phrase + verbal predicate: بخت ('fortune') modified by the adjective خندان ('smiling') and followed by شدست, an archaic/poetic past copula meaning 'became' (lit. 'it became'), so: 'fortune became smiling upon him' → 'fortune smiled on him.'
segments.s356-g34: پرستنده پاک یزدان شدست is a simple nominal phrase + past copula. پرستنده ('worshipper, devotee') + پاک ('pure') modifies it; یزدان ('God') is the object of worship (genitive relation implied): 'a pure worshipper of God.' شدست again is the archaic past copula 'became/was made.' Word order is adjective after noun (common in Persian poetry) and the copula follows the whole noun phrase.
segments.s356-g35: فرستاد is the simple past 3rd‑person verb 'he sent/dispatched' placed at the start. نزدیک تو is an adverbial phrase 'near you / to your presence' (نزدیک modifying the pronoun تو). تاج و گنج are the direct objects 'a crown and treasure' following the verb; Persian poetic word order often puts the verb before its objects without an object marker.
segments.s356-g36: سزد‌گر is a conditional/adversative compound: سزد (it is fitting) + -گر ('if/when') → 'if it be fitting' or 'if you think it right.' نداری functions here as 'lack/if you lack (them)' (the noun/idea of not having or implicitly 'you do not have'). کنون 'now' sets temporal focus; دل به رنج literally 'heart to suffering' is an idiom meaning 'to give the heart over to sorrow' — the phrase as a whole is an admonition: 'do not now give your heart to suffering.' Note the negative sense is implied in context (common in poetry) rather than marked by a negative particle.
segments.s356-g37: چنین گفت کایران is an introductory clause with inversion: چنین ('thus') + گفت ('said') + کایران (proper name) yields 'Thus said Kay Aran.' In classical/persian poetic word order the verb can precede or follow the speaker's name; گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the lemma list's 'conversation' sense is incidental—context shows it as speech verb).
segments.s356-g38: سر تخت با تاج کشور تراست is a verbless nominal clause with تراست (تَ + را + است) as the predicate meaning 'it is yours.' سر تخت ('the head/top of the throne') is a genitive/compound expression; با تاج کشور ('with the crown of the realm') further qualifies what is being granted. تراست = 'it is yours' (تَ = تو 'you' + را 'emphatic/obj' + است). The order places the possessed items before the copula, typical in Persian.
segments.s356-g39: ز گیتی یکی کنج ما را بس است is an existential/quantifying statement: ز گیتی ('of the world' / 'from the world') + یکی کنج ('one corner') forms the subject, and ما را بس است is a fixed idiom meaning 'is enough for us' (ما 'we/us' + را emphatic + بس 'enough' + است 'is'). Word order places the quantified noun phrase before the predicate 'is enough.'
segments.s356-g40: که here introduces an explanatory or causal clause 'for/that' (giving the reason). تخت مهی را = 'the moon‑like/splendid throne' (تخت 'throne' + مهی 'moon‑like/beautiful') with را marking the topical object. جز از من کس است literally 'except from me person is' — idiomatically 'there is no one for it except me' (جز ... کس = 'no one except ...'). The copula است closes the clause. Word order is common poetic inversion; 'کس' here functions as 'no one' in the restrictive construction.
segments.s356-g49: The clause is an exclamatory/performative phrase where خواندند (they called/cried) is the finite verb at the end, as typical in classical Persian. به شاهی بُرو uses به + abstract noun شاهی (“to/into kingship”) with برو (go) acting as a verbal idea of entering/becoming (here idiomatic), and آفرین is an acclamation (‘praise/hail’) that is the object/content of خواندند. Word order puts the destination/state (به شاهی) and the exclamation (آفرین) before the reporting verb خواندند.
segments.s356-g50: This is a coordinate clause continuing the report: ورا (to him) is the indirect object placed before the predicate; شهریار زمین is a title in noun-noun order (‘king/lord of the earth’), and خواندند repeats the reporting verb ‘they called/named’. The verb is again final and agrees with the plural announcers.
segments.s356-g51: ببودند is an older/palatalized form of بودند (they were) used here as a past-state verb; the following prepositional phrase بر پای بسته کمر describes that state: بر پای (on/at the foot) + بسته کمر (girdle/belt bound) — literally “with the belt tied at (or down to) the feet,” i.e. belt/girdle tightly bound around the waist/loins. Adjective/participial بسته modifies کمر.
segments.s356-g52: هرانکس که بودند is a protasis-like relative construction ‘whoever among them was…’; که is the relative/connector and بودند is the past-tense copula. پرخاشخر is a predicate/adjective describing those people (‘quarrelsome/aggressive’). The whole segment specifies which people were in the state just described.
segments.s356-g53: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction ‘when’; followed by the subject گشتاسپ and the past transitive verb دید ‘saw’. آن دلارای کام is the direct object: آن (that) + دلارای (beloved) + کام (desire), an epithet meaning ‘that beloved object of desire’. The phrase is compact and inverted: verb follows subject then object.
segments.s356-g54: فرستاد is the main past verb ‘(he) sent’; پیام is its direct object ‘a message’. نزدیک قیصر is an adverbial phrase ‘near (to) the Caesar’ indicating the recipient/place; in classical Persian such locative/adverbial phrases can follow the object. The clause means he dispatched a message to/near the Caesar.
segments.s356-g55: کز ایران can be read as ‘from/throughout Iran’ (کز = from/of), giving the scope; همه کام تو راست گشت is a clause where همه کام تو (‘all your wishes’) is the subject phrase and راست گشت (راست = true/fulfilled + گشت = became) is the past verbal phrase ‘were fulfilled/came true’. Word order places the scope phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s356-g56: سخنها (the talks/rumors) is the subject and ز اندازه اندر گذشت is a verbal predicate meaning ‘passed beyond measure’ — ز (from/beyond) + اندازه (measure/limit) + اندر (in/within, here adverbial) + گذشت (passed/exceeded). Together they express that the talk exceeded bounds — a common poetic formula.
segments.s356-g17: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. لشکر is the subject 'the army/host'. بدیدند is a simple past, 3rd person plural 'they saw'. گشتاسپ را is the definite object marked by را; the object follows the verb here (poetic word order) to complete the temporal clause 'when the army saw Gushtasp.'
segments.s356-g18: سرافرازتر is a comparative/adjectival predicate 'more honored/exalted' placed before the noun by poetic inversion; the fuller clause implies an omitted verbal idea like 'they made/honored (him)'. پور لهراسپ را is a noun phrase 'the son of Lohrasp' with را marking it as the (definite) patient/topic. The verb is omitted by ellipsis and supplied from context ('they honoured him'), which is common in epic diction.
segments.s356-g19: پیاده is an adverbial/description 'on foot' placed before the noun for emphasis. همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'before him' where اوی is an archaic/poetic form of 'او' ('him'). آمدند is 3rd person plural past 'they came'. The clause reads 'all on foot came before him.'
segments.s356-g20: This is a parallel descriptive clause built with two 'پر از ...' phrases: پر از درد 'full of pain/sorrow' and پر آب روی 'with faces full of water' (i.e. tearful). پر here means 'full (of)'; از introduces the source/'of'; روی (face) follows the modifying phrase, a normal noun-plus-adjective/order in poetry. آمدند repeats the verb 'they came,' completing the description.
segments.s356-g21: همه is the subject 'all'. پاک is adverbial here meaning 'purely/devoutly' modifying بردند. بردند (lit. 'they carried/brought') is used idiomatically for 'they offered' (a prayer). پیشش is 'before him' (پیش + enclitic ش for 'him'). نماز is the object 'prayer' and completes the verb phrase 'they offered prayer before him.'
segments.s356-g22: که here links result/cause ('and so/therefore'), introducing the outcome. کوتاه شد is a passive/resultative expression 'became short / were shortened' (شد as auxiliary/verb of becoming). رنجهای دراز is the subject phrase 'long sufferings' (رنجهای = sufferings, دراز = long) and follows the verb in poetic order. Together: 'and so the long sufferings were shortened.'
segments.s356-g23: همانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/just then'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. آمد is simple past 'came'. به پیشش is the prepositional phrase 'into/before him' (پیش + enclitic ش). زریر is the personal name; in this poetic word order the name follows the verb, giving emphasis: 'when Zareer came before him.'
segments.s356-g24: پیاده is 'on foot' (adverbial). ببود is an archaic past form of 'be' (3rd person) meaning 'was' (būd → bebūd in epic diction). و شد combines with شد 'and became' (3rd person past) to introduce a change of state. از رزم سیر literally 'sated/from battle' — سیر 'sated' with از marking the cause — meaning 'became weary/satiated of battle.' The subject 'he' (Zareer) is implicit throughout.
segments.s356-g25: گرامیش را تنگ در بر گرفت is an object (گرامیش + را) followed by a compound verb phrase. را marks گرامیش as the definite direct object. تنگ در بر گرفتن is an idiomatic construction meaning “to hold closely / to embrace tightly” (تنگ = closely, در + بر گرفتن = to take into one’s embrace). گرفت is the past-tense 3rd-person singular of گرفتن (“he took/seized”), so the whole segment reads “he clasped Garmish tightly in his arms.”
segments.s356-g26: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بگشاد is an archaic/perfect form with the b- prefix marking past (here “he opened”). لب is the noun “lip(s)” (object of بگشاد). پرسش اندر گرفت is a small clause where پرسش is the thing taken up and اندر گرفت (اندر + گرفتن) means “took into” in the sense of “began” or “took up” — i.e. “he began to ask / he opened his lips and began a question.” Word order is somewhat inverted/compact as is common in poetry.
segments.s356-g27: نشستند here is a past plural verb “they sat,” followed by the locative phrase بر تخت “on the throne.” با مهتران is a comitative phrase “with the mahteran (elders/chiefs).” Note: the lemma/senses list given for نشستند appears unrelated; grammatically this is a 3rd-person plural past action and the prepositional phrases follow the verb.
segments.s356-g28: This is a coordination: بزرگان ایران و کنداوران = “the great ones of Iran and the kandavaran.” بزرگان is a plural noun in the genitive construction with ایران (here meaning the land/realm of Iran). و simply connects the coordinated noun کنداوران (an agent-noun, here “excavators” or “diggers”).
segments.s356-g29: زریر خجسته به گشتاسپ گفت is a simple reporting clause: the proper name زریر with epithet خجسته (“auspicious/blessed”), then به marks the indirect object “to Gushtasp,” and گفت is the past 3rd-person singular “said.” The following که introduces reported speech.
segments.s356-g30: که introduces the content clause (“that ...”). بادی همه‌ساله is a nominal phrase “a wind, yearly / an annual wind” (همه‌ساله = yearly/annual). با بخت جفت is a prepositional phrase meaning “paired with fortune” (با = with, بخت = fortune, جفت = paired/mate used predicatively). Note that Persian often omits a copula, so the clause reads literally like “a yearly wind with fortune paired,” understood as “a yearly wind is paired with fortune.”
segments.s356-g31: پدر پیر سر شد تو برنادلی is vocative/statemental: پدر پیر سر شد literally “(the) father became head-old” — idiomatically “your father has grown old” or “the father became aged/gray-headed.” شد is the past of شدن “to become.” تو برنادلی here addresses the addressee (تو) and names him (برنادلی) in a vocative position; the order is poetic but means “O Bernadli, your father has grown old.”
segments.s356-g32: ز دیدار پیران چرا بگسلی is a question. ز here is the poetic preposition “from” (ز = از) and takes دیدار پیران “the meeting/visiting of the elders” as its object; چرا = “why.” بگسلی is a verbal form from گسستن/گسلیدن meaning “to break off / to separate,” here addressing the person (“why did you break away?”). The clause thus asks “Why did you break away from meeting the elders?”
segments.s356-g81: بشد is the past/copular form (ب + شد) meaning “became/it happened”; it stands before the subject (a poetic inversion). قیصر is the subject. The coordinated nouns رنج و تشویر (‘suffering and (an)xiety/worry’) follow, and برد is a second past verb (from بردن) with the same subject meaning “bore/experienced.” The two verbs licence a compound predicate: ‘Caesar became and bore suffering and anxiety.’ Note: تشویر here is an older/poetic spelling for the sense ‘worry/concern’.
segments.s356-g82: بسی is an adverb meaning ‘much/very’ (here rendered as ‘many’), and نیز ‘also’ adds another action. بر introduces the thing counted “against” something (بر خوی بد = against his bad disposition). خوی بد is the noun phrase ‘bad temper/character,’ and برشمرد is a past tense verb ‘he enumerated/count(ed).’ The object of برشمرد is elliptical (بسی = ‘many [things/faults]’).
segments.s356-g83: به سوی کتایون is a prepositional phrase ‘to/ toward Katayun’ (به + سوی + proper name). فرستاد is the simple past ‘sent’ (the subject is omitted/understood), and گنج is the direct object ‘treasure’ placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s356-g84: This is a short list of items specifying the treasure: یکی modifies افسر and means ‘one (helmet).’ The conjunction و links the two items. سرخ is an adjective ‘red’ qualifying یاقوت (ruby); the numeral پنج (five) applies to یاقوت and appears after it in the verse. The order is poetic but reads: ‘one helmet and five red rubies.’
segments.s356-g85: غلام و پرستار is a coordinated NP ‘slaves and attendants/caretakers.’ رومی qualifies them (‘Roman/Byzantine’). هزار (not listed among the words here) is the numeral ‘thousand’ that quantifies these people; its placement after the phrase is poetic but it counts the preceding nouns.
segments.s356-g86: یکی ‘one’ modifies طوق ‘necklace.’ پر is used here with the sense ‘full (of)’ (i.e., طوق full of something), followed by گوهر ‘gem/jewel’ as the thing filling it. شاهوار is an epithet meaning ‘regal/fit for a king.’ The phrase reads ‘one necklace, full of gems, kingly.’
segments.s356-g87: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of.’ دینار رومی = ‘Roman dinars’ (a sum of money). شتروار is a unit/measure related to camels (a camel-load); here it combines with the numeral پنج (five) to mean ‘five camel-loads’ — a poetic measure of quantity. Word order places the measure phrase before what follows.
segments.s356-g88: یکی ‘one’ introduces a single person: فیلسوفی in form would be expected to mean ‘a philosopher’ or learned man (the listed senses are abstract, so the specific ‘philosopher’ reading is the contextual noun). نگهبان گنج is a noun phrase ‘guardian/keeper of the treasure.’ Together: ‘one philosopher as the treasure’s guardian.’
segments.s356-g137: همه نیک بادا سرانجام تو is an optative clause: بادا is an old/poetic optative meaning “may it be.” همه نیک (= “entirely/wholly good”) modifies سرانجام (outcome), and تو is the possessor placed after the noun (literally “your outcome”). Word order here is adjective phrase + optative + possessed noun.
segments.s356-g138: مبادا که باشیم بی‌نام تو uses مبادا (a negative optative = “may it not be / lest”) followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. باشیم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/present (we be / we remain) used after مبادا, and بی‌نام is an adjective meaning “nameless”; تو again marks the possessor (literally “nameless to/with respect to you”).
segments.s356-g139: که introduces this explanatory/generalizing clause. The clause follows the common Persian order Subject (گیتی) + Verb (نماند) + adverb (همی); نماند here means “does not last / does not endure.” بر کسی means literally “upon/for anyone” (بر marking relation to a person). همی is an emphatic/continuous old form meaning “always/indeed.”
segments.s356-g140: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction “when.” ماند (first) is ‘remains’; به تن = “to/for the body” (به + تن). The second ماند repeats the verb to state what is left: “suffering remains.” بسی is a postpositive quantifier meaning “much / enough / plenty.” The clause gives a causal/resultive image: when only the body remains, much suffering remains.
segments.s356-g141: چنین است is an introductory formula “it is thus / such is it.” Here the predicate گیهان ناپایدار follows to state the message: ‘the world is inconstant/unstable.’ Note the classical/pastoral inversion where the copula است follows the demonstrative and the noun phrase follows to complete the thought.
segments.s356-g142: برو is an imperative (go / do so) used idiomatically to introduce the command that follows. تخم بد = “bad seed” (object), تا توانی = “as far as you can / to the extent you are able” (تا ... توانی = degree phrase), and مکار is a predicative adjective “deceitful” (an instruction: be deceitful). The clause is elliptical: the implied subject is second person (you).
segments.s356-g143: همی خواهم is a classical/habitual present: همی adds continuous/emphatic force to خواهم (“I desire / I ask”). از دادگر = “from the Just One” (از + epithet), and یک خدای is appositive ‘one God’ or ‘a single God’ — here an address to God as the just one. Word order places the requester and the addressee in a compact phrase.
segments.s356-g144: که introduces the content clause of the wish: چندان (to that extent / so long) modifies بمانم (subjunctive ‘that I may remain’ — a desiderative/subjunctive after خواهم). به گیتی = ‘in/within the world’ and به جای = ‘in (my) place / in position.’ The sequence expresses the speaker’s wish to endure in the world and retain his place/position.
segments.s356-g73: ازان کرده = a prepositional phrase 'from/for that deed' where 'ازان' (= 'از آن') points back to the prior action and 'کرده' is the perfective participle 'what (he) had done'. 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his disposition/act' (possessive formed by the enclitic ش). 'پوزش' is the object 'an apology', and 'گرفت' is simple past 'took' used idiomatically with 'پوزش' to mean 'asked for/received pardon.' Word order is literary: object phrase before the verb 'گرفت'.
segments.s356-g74: بپیچید is past 3rd singular of پیچیدن with the classical prefix ب- (common in older Persian), meaning 'he turned/withdrew'. زان (= 'از آن') = 'from/thereupon', introducing the phrase 'زان روزگار شگفت' = 'that wondrous time' (روزگار = time/fate; شگفت = wonderful/strange). The whole segment reports a following action: he turned aside/withdrew from that extraordinary time.
segments.s356-g75: بپذرفت is past 3rd singular 'he accepted' (the verb appears with a prefixed ب- as in classical usage). گفتار او = 'his speech/words' is the direct object placed immediately after the verb (poetic word order). شهریار (the subject 'the king') follows, a typical inversion in epic poetry where subject appears after verb+object.
segments.s356-g76: سرش را = 'his head' formed by noun سر + enclitic possessive ش and the object marker را. گرفت is past 'he seized/took' (from گرفتن) so 'سرش را گرفت' = 'he seized him by the head' or 'grabbed his head'. آنگهی = 'then/thereupon' is a temporal adverb, and برکنار here functions adverbially 'aside / to the side / put aside' (i.e. he then put/led him aside).
segments.s356-g77: بدو = 'to him' (compound of ب + او in older style) marking the indirect object; گفت here is the verb 'said' (the lemma senses list given is nominal, so verb sense is unlisted). چون تیره گردد هوا is a subordinate temporal/conditional clause: چون = 'when/as', تیره = 'dark', گردد is the present/subjunctive form of 'شدن/گشتن' meaning 'become' (literary third-person), and هوا = 'the air/sky' (i.e. the weather). Together: 'He said to him: when the sky becomes dark...'.
segments.s356-g78: فروزیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun 'to kindle/light' (used here as the action recommended). شمع = 'a candle' is its object. باشد is a present/subjunctive/formal copula meaning 'is' or 'may be' used to state a general judgment, and روا = 'fitting/permissible'. The clause states a general rule: 'it is fitting to light a candle.'
segments.s356-g79: بر ما فرست is an imperative phrase 'send to us' where بر + indirect object ما indicates direction/recipient and فرست is the imperative 'send'. آنک is a relative pronoun 'that/who/whom' introducing the relative clause; ما را گزید is the clause 'whom we chose' (word order: object-marker را follows ما, and گزید is past 'chose'). In poetry the relative clause follows the imperative, so the whole is a request: 'Send to us the one whom we have chosen.'
segments.s356-g80: که = 'for/that' introducing a causal/explanatory clause. او = 'he' (subject). درد و رنج = 'pain and suffering' (a fixed pair; درد = physical/emotional pain, رنج = hardship/suffering). فراوان modifies them 'much/abundant'. کشید is past of کشیدن 'to bear/undergo' (here 'he suffered/bore'). Together: 'for he suffered much pain and hardship.'
segments.s356-g105: سپه (subject) + سوی ایران (directional prepositional phrase) comes before the verbal complex. برفتن is an infinitive (بـ + رفتن) meaning 'to depart/move', and گرفت (past of گرفتن) here functions as a light verb meaning 'took to / began (to...)' — together برفتن گرفت = 'began to depart'. Word order (subject → direction → verb cluster) is typical of epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s356-g106: هوا گرد is a nominal phrase where گرد is used as 'dust' (a dust‑cloud/wind), acting as the subject. اسپان (horses) is the object of نهفتن 'to hide'; نهفتن گرفت mirrors the previous verbal pattern (infinitive + گرفت) and means 'began to hide'. So the clause literally says 'a dust‑cloud began to hide the horses.'
segments.s356-g107: چو = 'when'. قیصر is the subject. دو منزل is a numeral + noun ('two stages' — منزل as a stage of travel), and بیامد is the past tense 'came/arrived'. به راه is an adverbial phrase 'on the road/on the way' placed after the verb. The phrase means 'when Caesar had come two stages on the road.'
segments.s356-g108: عنان (the reins) is the direct object, تگاور ('rider/warrior') marks the goal/direction of the action, and بپیچید is the past tense 'turned' (the prefixed form with بـ is common in classical/poetic Persian). The subject شاه appears after the verb (verb–subject order is frequent in poetry) — 'the king turned the reins toward the rider.'
segments.s356-g109: به سوگند functions as an adverbial of means ('by/with an oath' — به here marks the instrument/means). ازان مرز = 'from that border/frontier' (ازان = 'from that'). برگاشتش is a past verb with the enclitic object -ش ('him/it'); برگاشت here means 'caused to return / turned back.'
segments.s356-g110: به خواهش = 'at the request (of him)' (به marks the circumstance/means again), سوی روم is the directional phrase 'toward Rome', and بگذاشتش is past + enclitic object 'he let/placed/sent him'. بگذاشت here carries the sense 'permitted/sent' (he let him go toward Rome).
segments.s356-g111: وزان = 'and/from that' (linking back), جایگه 'place/station' and شد (past of شدن) form an idiomatic motion/result clause; here شد + directional phrase means 'set out / departed'. سوی روم باز = 'again toward Rome' where باز = 'again' (postposed for emphasis). Overall: 'From that place he again set out toward Rome.'
segments.s356-g112: چو = 'when'; گشتاسپ is the subject (a proper name). شد again functions as the past verb of motion/turning, and سوی راه دراز = 'toward the long road' where دراز is a postpositive adjective modifying راه (road). The clause means 'when Gushtasp turned/sent (his course) toward the long road.'
segments.s356-g9: برآرم is a first‑person form (classical-present/subjunctive used for a future/intentional sense) meaning “I will lift/wrest/bring up.” از ایشان is contracted here as ازیشان = “from them.” همه کام تو is a genitive phrase ‘all (the) desire(s) of/for you’ (همه = all, کام = wish/desire, تو = you); the direct object of برآرم is the whole phrase 'از ایشان همه کام تو'.
segments.s356-g10: درفشان کنم combines a descriptive element (درفشان 'shining, glittering') with کنم, the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used here to mean 'I will make/cause.' در جهان = 'in the world,' and نام تو = 'your name' (name/fame) — together: 'I will make your name shine in the world.' Word order places the verb (کنم) after the modifier (درفشان) in typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s356-g11: بدو گفت قیصر = 'Caesar said to him' with بدو = 'to him' (dative/enclitic), گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (classical narrative word order places the indirect object/particle before گفت), قیصر = 'Caesar/emperor', and تو داناتری is a direct address 'you are wiser' (تو = you, داناتری = comparative 'more knowing/wiser').
segments.s356-g12: برین آرزو is a demonstrative phrase (برین = this/that (poetic formation), آرزو = 'wish/desire'). بر ... تواناتری here links the comparative: بر + تواناتری ≈ 'with/regarding (it) you are more capable/stronger' — i.e. 'you are more able in this matter.' The preposition بر is used poetically to mean 'concerning/regarding' and تواناتری is a comparative adjective 'more able/stronger'.
segments.s356-g13: چو بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'), گشتاسپ is the subject 'Gushtasp'. گفتار اوی = 'his speech/words' with گفتار = 'speech' and اوی a classical possessive enclitic meaning 'his/her/its' (here 'his'). The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When Gushtasp heard his speech...'.
segments.s356-g14: نشست is here the verb 'he sat (down)' or 'took a seat.' از بر باره is a compact classical phrasing meaning 'about/with respect to the matter' (از بر + باره = 'concerning the case/occasion'), and راه جوی is a verbal‑noun compound meaning 'seeking a way' (راه = way/path, جوی = seeking/search). Together the clause means he sat down to think and seek a way regarding the matter.
segments.s356-g15: بیامد = 'came' (past), به جای نشست = 'sat in (his) place' or 'took his place' (به جای = to/in the place, نشست = sat). زریر at the end names the person who performed the action; classical Persian often places the proper name after the verb cluster.
segments.s356-g16: This segment lists Zarir's equipment: به سر افسر = 'a helmet on (his) head' (به سر = on the head, افسر = helmet), و = 'and', بادپایی = 'light/fleet‑footed footwear' or 'light shoes' (an epithet often applied to light boots or a swift runner; here descriptive of his footgear), به زیر = 'beneath' (underfoot). The whole phrase qualifies how he was seated (with helmet on head and light footwear underfoot).
segments.s356-g41: Word order is subject–verb–object (classical Persian SOV): برادر (subject) + بیاورد (verb) + پرمایه تاج (object). بیاورد is the classical/narrative past (or third‑person perfective) corresponding to آورد ‘brought’ (an archaic/poetic form). پرمایه is a prefixed adjective meaning ‘substantial/sumptuous’ modifying تاج ‘crown’.
segments.s356-g42: A nominal list introduced by the demonstrative همان ‘that/the same’. همـ and و mark coordination: همان یاره ‘that very yāreh’, و طوق ‘and a collar’, و هم تخت عاج ‘and also an ivory throne’. هم before تخت strengthens the additive sense (‘also’). طوق is a jewelry noun; عاج is an adjective/noun meaning ‘ivory’, placed after تخت as a qualifier.
segments.s356-g43: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’. The clause follows canonical order: subject گشتاسپ, object تختِ پدر (throne of the father; genitive relation), verb دید ‘saw’. شاد is a predicative adjective ‘glad/joyful’ that links to the following verb (شاد نشست = ‘sat down joyful’); the full verbal idea continues in the next segment.
segments.s356-g44: نشست is the past ‘he sat (down)’. از introduces a source/partitive relation ‘from, out of’ and combines with برش (here used poetically as ‘from its side/from it’) to indicate taking the crown from the throne; تاج بر سر نهاد is a compact clause: تاج (object) + بر سر (prepositional phrase ‘on [his] head’) + نهاد (past of نهادن ‘placed’). نهاد is therefore the main transitive past verb ‘placed’.
segments.s356-g45: نبیره is the head noun ‘scion/offspring’. It is followed by the attributive compound جهانجوی ‘world‑seeking’ (an epithet) and then the possessor کاوس (Kāvus), so the phrase reads ‘the scion of world‑seeking Kāvus’. The final کی is a relative/particle ‘who’ linking this noun phrase to what follows.
segments.s356-g46: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/among’. گودرزیان names the group ‘the Gudarzis’. هرک is an indefinite ‘whoever/anyone’; here it introduces a generalizing clause. بد and نیک‌پی form a coordinated contrast ‘bad (ill‑) … well‑born’ (i.e. ‘whether ill‑ or well‑born’), with نیک‌پی ‘well‑born’ used as a lineage/quality adjective.
segments.s356-g47: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘as/when’. A string of examples follows: بهرام (proper name) coordinated with چون ساوه (‘like Saveh’) and و ریونیز (‘and Riyoniz’). The repeated use of چون/چو is a poetic device introducing comparison/examples. ریونیز here functions as a named exemplar (an uncertain/archaic name in the text).
segments.s356-g48: کسی is the indefinite ‘anyone/someone’. In older Persian کو can function as a relative particle ‘who’ (roughly = کسی که). سرافراز بودند is the past plural ‘they/they (those people) were exalted/triumphant’; بودند is the plural past of بودن. نیز at the end reinforces the additive/also sense (‘likewise’). The phrase expresses that people comparable to those named were likewise exalted.
segments.s356-g89: سلیح و درم are the direct objects (a nominative list of goods: 'arms and dirhams') placed before the verb, a common poetic word order. داد is the past-tense verb 'gave'; لشکرش را (his army + object marker را) is the (definite) recipient. The verb thus governs both the preposed goods and the following marked recipient.
segments.s356-g90: همان here is a pronominal demonstrative meaning 'the same' (referring back to the gifts just named). نامداران کشورش را is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned men of his country' marked by را, showing they are the other definite recipients of the same gifts; the verb (داد) is omitted here by ellipsis, carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s356-g91: هرانکس که بود او is a relative/indefinite clause 'whoever he was'; که introduces the clause and بود is the past copula 'was'. او is a resumptive pronoun reinforcing the person. ز تخم بزرگ is a prepositional phrase 'from a great seed/stock/lineage' (ز = 'from' in poetic register) modifying his origin.
segments.s356-g92: وگر is the conditional 'or if' (introducing an alternative description). تیغ‌زن نامداری is a noun phrase 'a renowned swordsman' (تیغ‌زن = 'swordsman', نامداری = 'renowned' used adjectivally). The final word سترگ is an archaic/poetic adjective here intensifying 'نامداری' ('great'/'mighty'); the verb is elided (understood from the previous بود).
segments.s356-g93: بیاراست is a past verb meaning 'he adorned' or 'he made (them) beautiful.' خلعت is the object 'robe(s) of honour' and سزاوارشان is an adjective phrase 'fitting/appropriate for them' (سزاوار + possessive -شان). Together they mean 'he adorned them with robes befitting them.'
segments.s356-g94: برافرخت is a past verb here meaning 'he roused/caused to revive' (poetic causative use). پژمرده بازارشان 'their faded/withered market' is the affected noun phrase (پژمرده = 'withered/faded'; بازارشان = 'their market/bazaar'). The clause says he enlivened or revived their once-faded marketplace; the verb is transitive on بازارشان.
segments.s356-g95: از is the poetic preposition 'from.' اسپان تازی is a genitive/attributive sequence 'Arabian horses' (اسپان = horses, تازی = Arabian/Arabian-horse). و connects the next item; برگستوان here appears as a lexical item in the list but is archaic/ambiguous in meaning in this context (the form could be a noun or a verbal noun). Grammatically this whole segment is a part of a catalog introduced by از.
segments.s356-g96: ز (again 'from') begins the next part of the same list. خفتان is an archaic or poetic word here (the provided sense list gives 'to sleep' but that verbal sense doesn't fit well as a list-item noun), so its exact lexical sense in this line is uncertain. وز is an old/poetic connective (here functioning like 'and' or a resumed 'and/from'), جامه هندوان means 'garments of the Indians' (جامه = garment, هندوان = Indians). The segment is therefore another 'from X and from Y' phrase; several items are lexicalized or archaic and so ambiguous.
segments.s356-g97: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing a list of items; the nouns are simply coordinated with 'و' ('and') between them, so the whole phrase is a single enumerated prepositional phrase (no verb in this segment). Word order is noun phrase list after the preposition: ز + [noun (و noun)*].
segments.s356-g98: The preposition 'ز' repeats for rhetorical balance: 'ز تخت' = 'from the throne', then 'ز هرگونه دیبای چین' = 'from every kind of Chinese brocade.' 'هرگونه' ('any/ every kind') modifies 'دیبای چین' (brocade of China), with 'چین' functioning as a postmodifier ('Chinese').
segments.s356-g99: Here 'برد' is the past tense of بردن 'to take/bring' (3rd singular), with 'فرستاده' as its object and 'نزدیک گشتاسپ' as an adverbial phrase of place ('to/near Gushtasp'). Word order is object + adverbial + verb, which is normal in older Persian narrative.
segments.s356-g100: 'یکایک' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn'; 'به' marks direction or goal ('into/to') and combines with 'گنجور او' = 'his treasury' (the pronoun 'او' following the noun indicates possession). 'برشمرد' is the simple past ('he counted, he enumerated').
segments.s356-g101: 'این بسی' is a quantitative phrase 'this much/so great an amount'; 'آفرین گسترید' is a verb phrase where 'آفرین' (acclamation/praise) is the object and 'گسترید' (past of گستراندن) means 'spread/uttered/extended' — together 'he heaped/uttered praise.' The initial 'ابا' is archaic here and not functioning as the usual 'to refuse'; it appears as an exclamatory/introductory particle (sense unclear or archaic).
segments.s356-g102: 'بران' = 'upon that/unto him' (archaic contraction of بر آن), followed by the relative-like particle 'کو' here used as 'who/that' linking to the verb 'آفرید' (created). 'زمان و زمین آفرید' is the clause 'created time and earth' with straightforward word order: object(s) then verb.
segments.s356-g103: The subordinating conjunction 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause: subject 'کتایون' + 'چو آمد' ('when [she] came'), then the directional phrase 'به نزدیک شاه' ('to/near the king') where 'به' marks direction and 'نزدیک شاه' specifies location.
segments.s356-g104: Noun-noun collocation 'غو کوس' ('the tumult/noise of the drum') functions as the subject of the intransitive verb 'برخاست' ('arose/resounded'); 'از بارگاه' ('from the palace/court') is a source phrase introduced by 'از'.
segments.s356-g1: چو is a temporal subordinating particle meaning 'when' (introducing a time clause). برخاست is a past tense verb 'arose/rose' placed immediately after چو in classical/poetic word order. قیصر is the subject (Caesar/emperor). به + proper name (به گشتاسپ) marks the indirect object/recipient 'to Gushasp'. گفت is the main past-tense verb 'said' concluding the clause; the sequence is a typical classical Persian order: temporal clause → subject → indirect-object phrase → verb.
segments.s356-g2: که introduces the reported speech clause ('that' / quotative). پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply' and is the theme of the question. چرا is the interrogative 'why'. ماندی is 2nd-person past 'you remained/you kept' (here 'you kept/left'). در نهفت is a prepositional phrase: در 'in' + نهفت 'hidden/concealed', giving 'kept hidden' or 'left in concealment'. Word order is typical: [object/theme] + [interrogative] + [verb] + [locative complement].
segments.s356-g3: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (i.e., 'to him said...'); گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (poetic past), and گشتاسپ is the speaker's name (here placed after گفت in inverted/order variation typical of epic diction). من is the first-person pronoun 'I'. پیش ازین is a compound adverb meaning 'previously / before this' (پیش 'before' + از + ین as orthographic element), so the clause sets up a past state.
segments.s356-g4: ببودم is an archaic/past perfect-type form meaning 'I had been' or 'I was formerly' (intensive/preterite sense). بر with a noun of rule here means 'over' / 'as ruler of' (i.e., indicating authority). شاه ایران‌زمین is the titular phrase 'the shah (king) of the land of Iran' (ایران‌زمین = the Iranian realm). Together the phrase expresses a former status: 'I had been king over Iran.'
segments.s356-g5: همه is a universal quantifier 'all' placed before the noun phrase. لشکر شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's army' (لشکر = host/army; شاه governs it). و آن انجمن adds 'and that assembly/council', forming a coordinated subject phrase. This segment lists the groups who are aware.
segments.s356-g6: همه (again) quantifies the verb phrase: 'all (of them)'. آگهند is a third-person plural present/stative verb 'they know/are aware' agreeing with the plural subject. از introduces the object of knowledge ('of/regarding'), and هنرهای من is 'my arts/skills/deeds' (هنرهای = plural 'skills/arts'; من = 'my'). The clause thus means 'all of them know of my skills.'
segments.s356-g7: همان به که is an idiomatic evaluative phrase meaning 'it is best/appropriate that' (همان here functioning as 'that'/'the same' in the idiom). من is the first-person pronoun. سوی ایشان is a directional phrase 'toward them / to their presence' (سوی = toward; ایشان = them). شوم is the 1st-person singular subjunctive/present-tense form 'I should become/go' used here as a subjunctive of intention/recommendation; the whole segment expresses a proposal or resolution: 'it is best that I go to them.'
segments.s356-g8: بگویم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present 'I should say / I will say' (expressing intention). The omitted conjunction of purpose is typical in Persian; the sequence links to بشنوم as a purpose/result clause. همه گفته‌ها is the object 'all the statements/reports' (گفته‌ها = plural 'things said' / 'statements'). بشنوم is the 1st-person subjunctive/present of 'hear' used after بگویم to express the intended result or expectation 'so that I may hear' or 'and I will hear.' The pair expresses 'I shall speak and hear all that is said.'
segments.s356-g121: فرود آمد is a compound past: فرود (a noun used here as verbal-adverbial 'descent/alighting') + آمد 'came/arrived', together 'descended/came down'. از باره is a prepositional phrase 'از' + باره 'on the occasion of / for the sake of', so از باره گشتاسپ means 'for Gushtasp's sake' (poetic word order places the prepositional phrase before the adverb زود). زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying آمد.
segments.s356-g122: بدو is 'to him' (indirect object). آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (an exclamation/commendation) + کرد 'did/made' → 'he praised/acclaimed (him)'. و is the coordinating 'and'. زاری نمود is another light-verb pairing: زاری 'lamentation' + نمود (past of نمودن 'to show/express') → 'he bewailed/expressed lamentation'.
segments.s356-g123: ز ره is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the road/way'. چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when'. به + ایوان شاهی indicates motion toward 'to the royal portico/hall' (ایوان شاهی = 'royal portico/hall'). شدند is the past plural of شدن 'they became/arrived' used here to state their arrival/being at that place.
segments.s356-g124: چو at the start of this clause is a comparative/simile 'as/like'. خورشید is the noun 'sun' used as the image for radiance. در برج ماهی literally 'in the sign/tower of Pisces' (برج here = zodiac sign; ماهی = 'fish' i.e. Pisces). شدند again is past plural 'they became' → 'they became/appeared like the sun in the Pisces sign' (a poetic image of brilliance).
segments.s356-g125: بدو گفت لهراسپ is inverted word order typical of classical Persian: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'said' + لهراسپ (the speaker) → 'Lohrasp said to him'. کز introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'which'); من is 'me'. مبین here is an archaic/poetic use: literally 'manifest/clear', but in context it functions to warn the addressee not to take/attribute the matter to the speaker (i.e. 'do not consider this as from me').
segments.s356-g126: چنین 'thus/so' + بود (past of بودن) = 'was so'. رای here means 'counsel/plan/advice' and جهان‌آفرین is 'the World‑Creator' (a name/title for God). Together, the clause states the reason/explanation: 'such was the Creator's counsel.'
segments.s356-g127: نوشته used nominatively/adverbially ('it is written' / 'written') + چنین 'thus' = 'it is written thus'. بد here carries the sense 'ill/bad' and مگر functions as 'unless' or 'except'. بر سرت literally 'upon your head' is an idiom meaning 'be a misfortune for you' (a cautionary phrase placed parenthetically).
segments.s356-g128: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. پردخت ('payment/tribute') is the subject of this clause. ماند (past of ماندن) 'remained/will remain' gives the outcome. ز تو 'from/of you' indicates source/possession, and کشورت 'your kingdom' is the genitive possessor; together the clause says that the tribute of your realm will remain (i.e. continue to be due/kept to you).
segments.s356-g145: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that', linking this clause to the preceding desiderative verb ('همی خواهم'). این نامه شهریاران پیش is a noun phrase without an explicit verb (poetic omission of copula): این 'this' + نامه 'book/record' + شهریاران 'of the kings' (genitive relationship expressed by juxtaposition rather than ezafe in poetry) + پیش functioning as the postpositional/adverbial 'before / in the presence of'. The clause therefore sets up the object/place ('this book before the kings') which the verb in the next segment will act on.
segments.s356-g146: بپیوندم is a first-person optative/subjunctive form 'may I join/attach/present' (used after که to express the speaker's wish). از introduces the means/agent ('by/through'), so از خوب‌گفتار خویش means 'by/through (one's) eloquent speech'. خوب‌گفتار is a compound adjective/noun 'well-spoken / eloquent', and خویش is the reflexive/possessive item ('one's own' / 'my own' in context), here written as خوی + enclitic ش; together they mark possession of the eloquence. Grammatically the verb بپیوندم takes the preceding noun phrase ('این نامه شهریاران پیش') as its object/place and the phrase introduced by از as the means.
segments.s356-g147: ازان پس is an adverbial frame 'after that / thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after'), introducing the following statement. تن جانور خاک is a compact, poetic noun phrase in which nouns are juxtaposed without an explicit ezafe: تن 'the body' + جانور 'an animal/creature' + خاک 'of dust' (i.e. 'a creature of dust'). راست at the end is a predicative word 'is true / indeed so' serving as the copula/predication in this line (typical in epic poetry to place the predicate at the end).
segments.s356-g148: سخن گوی is the split form of the compound 'سخن‌گوی' meaning 'the one who speaks' (here 'the speaking' or 'speaker'); in poetry compounds are often separated. جان here is 'soul' (subject) so سخن گوی جان reads 'the soul that speaks' or 'the speaking soul'. معدن پاک راست is a predicate phrase: معدن 'mine / source' + پاک 'pure' and راست again functioning as 'is true / indeed so'. Like the previous segment, the copula is omitted and راست at the end affirms the predicate. Overall the two half-lines make a parallel statement: the body is a dusty animal, whereas the soul that speaks is a pure source.
segments.s356-g129: بدو + شادمان گشت is a verb‑phrase meaning “became glad at him/with him.” In this classical order the verb گشت (past of گشتن, “became”) follows the predicate شادمان; لهراسپ شاه (subject) comes after the verb for emphasis: “Lohrasp the king became glad at him.”
segments.s356-g130: مر here is the 1st‑person object “me” (archaic separate form), so مر او را نشاند = “he seated him by/next to me.” نشاند is the past transitive “set/placed/caused to sit.” از بر تخت و گاه is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning “upon the throne and (in the) chamber/seat” (از بر + تخت = on the throne; گاه = place/hall).
segments.s356-g131: ببوسید is a past verb “(he) kissed” with the (previously mentioned) object implied. تاجش = “his crown” (تاج + possessive ـش). به سر is the locative “on (the) head.” بر نهاد (literary compound of بر + نهاد) is the past of نهادن, “placed upon.” Word order places the object phrase between verb and verb‑complement.
segments.s356-g132: همی is an older adverb meaning “continually/ever” or an emphatic marker; آفرین here is the acclamation “bravo/praise” (not the present stem), and کرد is the auxiliary/past “did/sounded.” با تاج یاد = “with the crown (as) remembrance/commemoration” (یاد = remembrance). The clause means he kept praising/acclaiming while the crown was given/remembered.
segments.s356-g133: بدو گفت is the literal “(he) said to him” (بدو = to him; گفت here functions as the verb “said” — word‑order is verb + indirect object). گشتاسپ is the speaker’s name placed after the verb; کای شهریار is a vocative address: کای = “O” (vocative/title) + شهریار = “king/lord.”
segments.s356-g134: This is an optative/jussive clause. مبیناد is an older 3rd‑person form meaning “may ... appear/be seen” (optative). تو = “you.” کس روزگار = “anyone of the world/fate” (کس = anyone/person; روزگار = time/fortune). Together the clause expresses a wish along the lines of “may no one in the world equal you in fortune.” Note: ابی is an archaic/exclamatory element here (vocative/intensifier) rather than the literal color “blue.”
segments.s356-g135: چو here = “if.” مهتر کنی is “(you) make (me) greater/exalt (me)” (کنی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used causatively), with the object (me) omitted but implied. من ترا کهترم = “I am lesser than you” — ترا (to/than you) is the comparative marker and کهترم = 1st‑person “I am inferior.” The clause is a humble self‑deprecation: even if you exalt me, I remain lower than you.
segments.s356-g136: بکوشم is 1st‑person present/jussive “I will strive/try.” که is the conjunction “that/so that.” گرد here is used in the sense “to/into the keeping of” (classical usage “into the circle/charge of”), ت + را = the 2nd‑person object “you” with the object marker. نسپرم is ن‑ (negative) + سپرم (1st‑person of سپردن “to entrust/hand over”) = “I will not entrust/hand over.” Together: “I will strive that I do not hand you over to (abandon you to) fate/world.”
segments.s356-g65: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. گشتاسپ را دید is a past-tense clause 'he saw Gushtasp' with the object marked by را; the subject is omitted (understood from context). بر تخت عاج is a locative phrase 'on the ivory throne' that describes where Gushtasp was seen.
segments.s356-g66: نهاده is a past participle 'having placed/placed' used adjectivally; به سر 'on the head' marks the position, and the following phrase بر ز پیروزه تاج means 'a crown of turquoise upon (it/his head)'. The word order is poetic (participle + position + noun phrase) and the prepositions emphasize the placement of the تاج.
segments.s356-g67: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. ورا is the enclitic form 'to him / at him' (object/goal). تنگ در برگرفت is an idiomatic compound: تنگ 'close/tightly' + در (part of the verbal compound) + برگرفت (past of برگرفتن) giving 'embraced/took him tightly' — the verb is transitive and the adverb precedes it for emphasis.
segments.s356-g68: سخنهای دیرینه is a noun phrase 'the old/long-standing words(speeches)' (سخنهای = plural/construct), and اندر گرفت is another verbal idiom 'to take up' (گرفت past) meaning here 'began to speak of' or 'took up the subject'. The verb گرفت governs the preceding noun phrase as its object/topic.
segments.s356-g69: بدانست is past 'he knew/realized'. The subject قیصر follows the verb by poetic inversion (normally قیصر بدانست). که introduces the subordinate content clause. گشتاسپ اوست is a predicative clause 'Gushtasp is he' (اوست = 'he is'), here identifying the person.
segments.s356-g70: فروزنده جان is a nominal phrase 'the life‑brightener / reviver of life' (فروزنده as agentive/participial adjective + جان 'life'). لهراسپ follows as a genitival/name identifying whose life is brightened (i.e., 'of Lohrasp'). اوست is the copula 'is (he)'. Together the phrase apposes/identifies the person by title.
segments.s356-g71: فراوانش is فراوان with the enclitic -ش 'him' appended, meaning 'him abundantly / lavishly'. بستود is past 'praised'. و is the conjunction 'and'. بردش نماز places the noun نماز ('prayer') before the verb برد (here an idiomatic sense 'to offer/perform') with the clitic -ش 'him' attached: idiomatically 'offered prayer for him' or 'performed prayers on his behalf'.
segments.s356-g72: وزانجا is a fused conjunctive‑locative 'and from there'. سوی تخت is the directional phrase 'toward the throne'. رفتند is the past plural 'they went' and باز postposed means 'back/again' — together 'they went back toward the throne.'

sentences.s356-s29: Zarir and the host keep watch, expecting that you will come proudly into this battlefield.
sentences.s356-s30: Everyone, wholly, will make a covenant with you and pledge their souls as hostages to your love.
sentences.s356-s31: If no suffering befalls you as you stride proudly across the plain, then the affairs of the age have turned to your favour.
sentences.s356-s32: When the envoy reached Caesar's presence, he came out to the plain and saw the army's martial music/array.
sentences.s356-s57: He rode on until he reached Iran and came before the brave and the lion‑hearted.
sentences.s356-s58: When Lohrasp heard, Zarir came — his brother Gushasp, that manly lion.
sentences.s356-s59: He was received by all the chiefs — the great men of Iran and the renowned heroes.
sentences.s356-s60: When he saw his son he took him in his arms; because of the cruelty of Fate he placed his hand on his head.
sentences.s356-s17: In his old age fortune smiled upon him; he became a pure worshipper of God.
sentences.s356-s18: He sent a crown and treasure to you; if it is fitting — if you lack them — do not now afflict your heart with sorrow.
sentences.s356-s19: Thus Kay Aran said: 'All of it is yours.'
sentences.s356-s20: One corner of the world is enough for us; as for the splendid (moon‑like) throne, there is no one for it except me.
sentences.s356-s25: They acclaimed him to kingship and called him the sovereign of the earth.
sentences.s356-s26: Whoever among them was quarrelsome had his girdle tied at his waist (i.e. stood with his belt bound).
sentences.s356-s27: When Gushtasp saw that beloved object of desire, he sent a message to Caesar nearby.
sentences.s356-s28: From Iran, all your wishes were fulfilled; the talk exceeded measure.
sentences.s356-s9: When the army saw Gushtasp, they honored the son of Lohrasp all the more.
sentences.s356-s10: All on foot came before him; they came full of pain and with faces wet with tears.
sentences.s356-s11: They all devoutly offered prayer before him, and thus their long sufferings were shortened.
sentences.s356-s12: At that very moment, when Zareer came before him, he was on foot and had grown weary of battle.
sentences.s356-s13: He drew Garmish tightly into his arms; when he opened his lips, he began to ask.
sentences.s356-s14: They sat on the throne with the mahteran, the great men of Iran and the excavators.
sentences.s356-s15: Zarir the Auspicious said to Gushtasp that each year a wind is paired with (fortune).
sentences.s356-s16: Your father has become old, O Bernadli; why did you break away from meeting the elders?
sentences.s356-s41: Caesar became beset by suffering and anxiety, and he also counted many things against his bad temper.
sentences.s356-s42: He sent treasure to Katayun: one helmet and five red rubies.
sentences.s356-s43: A thousand Roman slaves and attendants, and one necklace full of jewels fit for a king.
sentences.s356-s44: Five camel-loads of Roman dinars, and one philosopher as the guardian of the treasure.
sentences.s356-s69: May all your outcomes be good; may it not be that we are nameless to you.
sentences.s356-s70: For the world does not endure for anyone; when only the body remains, much suffering remains.
sentences.s356-s71: Such is the world: unstable. Go — sow bad seed; be deceitful as much as you can.
sentences.s356-s72: I continually desire from the just One, God, that I may remain so long in the world and in my place.
sentences.s356-s37: He apologized for what he had done, and withdrew from that wondrous time.
sentences.s356-s38: The king accepted his words; he seized him by the head and then put him aside.
sentences.s356-s39: He said to him, 'When the air grows dark, it is fitting to light a candle.'
sentences.s356-s40: Send to us the one whom we have chosen, for he has endured much pain and hardship.
sentences.s356-s53: The army began to move toward Iran; a cloud of dust rose and hid the horses.
sentences.s356-s54: When the Caesar had advanced two stages on the road, the king turned the reins toward the rider.
sentences.s356-s55: By an oath he made him return from that frontier; at his request he sent (let) him toward Rome.
sentences.s356-s56: From that place he again set out toward Rome; when Gushtasp set out toward the long road.
sentences.s356-s5: I will wrest from them all that you desire, and I will make your name shine in the world.
sentences.s356-s6: Caesar said to him, 'You are wiser; you are more capable with regard to this desire.'
sentences.s356-s7: When Gushtasp heard his words, he sat down to consider and seek a way about the matter.
sentences.s356-s8: Zarir came and sat in his place, with a helmet on his head and light footwear beneath (his feet).
sentences.s356-s21: His brother brought the sumptuous crown, that same yāreh and a collar, and also an ivory throne.
sentences.s356-s22: When Gushtasp saw his father's throne he rejoiced and sat down; from it he placed the crown upon his head.
sentences.s356-s23: The scion — the world‑seeking Kāvus’s offspring — whoever of the Gudarzis, whether ill‑ or well‑born.
sentences.s356-s24: As Bahram, and like Saveh and Riyoniz, there was likewise no one who was not exalted.
sentences.s356-s45: He gave arms and dirhams to his army — the same to the famed men of his realm.
sentences.s356-s46: Whoever he was, sprung from a great stock — or, if (he was) a famed swordsman of great renown.
sentences.s356-s47: He decked them in robes befitting them and revived their faded market.
sentences.s356-s48: From Arabian horses and returning mounts; from khuftān (uncertain/archaic item) and from the garments of the Indians.
sentences.s356-s49: From brocades and dinars and crowns and gems; from the throne and from every kind of Chinese brocade.
sentences.s356-s50: He took the messenger near Gushtasp; he counted them one by one into his treasury.
sentences.s356-s51: He poured out so much praise upon Him who created time and earth.
sentences.s356-s52: When Katayun came near the king, a tumult of drums rose from the palace.
sentences.s356-s1: When the Caesar rose, he said to Gushasp, "Why did you keep the answer hidden?"
sentences.s356-s2: Gushasp said to him, "I had formerly been (the) king over the land of Iran."
sentences.s356-s3: All the king's army and that assembly — all know of my skills.
sentences.s356-s4: It is better that I go to them; I will speak and hear all the statements.
sentences.s356-s61: He quickly descended for Gushtasp's sake; he praised him and bewailed.
sentences.s356-s62: When they came from the road to the royal portico, they shone like the sun in the sign of Pisces.
sentences.s356-s63: Lohrasp said to him, 'Do not attribute this to me; such was the Creator's counsel.'
sentences.s356-s64: It is thus written — unless it prove ill for you — that the tribute of your realm shall remain with you.
sentences.s356-s73: that I may present this book before the kings by my eloquent speech.
sentences.s356-s74: After that: the body is truly an animal of dust; the speaking soul is truly a pure mine.
sentences.s356-s65: King Lohrasp became joyful at him; he seated him beside me upon the throne and in the hall.
sentences.s356-s66: He kissed him and set the crown upon his head; he repeatedly cried “bravo” with the crown of remembrance.
sentences.s356-s67: Gushtasp said to him, “O king, may no one in the world be like you in fortune.”
sentences.s356-s68: If you exalt me, I am still inferior to you; I will strive that I do not hand you over to (or abandon you to) fate.
sentences.s356-s33: When he saw Gushtasp on the ivory throne, with a turquoise crown placed upon his head.
sentences.s356-s34: He came to him and embraced him tightly, and then took up old words (began to speak of ancient matters).
sentences.s356-s35: Caesar realized that this was Gushtasp — he was the life-brightener of Lohrasp.
sentences.s356-s36: He praised him lavishly and offered prayers for him; then from there they returned again toward the throne.
